insights: Incidents are perfectly uniformly distributed across all five categories—Database, Hardware, Inquiry / Help, Software, and Network—with exactly 100 incidents assigned to each, indicating zero discrepancy in categorical distribution.
Assignment load is equally balanced across all five assignees, with each handling exactly 100 incidents, confirming no resource imbalance exists at the individual assignment level.

The absence of missing values in category and assignee fields, combined with exact integer splits, confirms the uniformity is structural and intentional—not an artifact of data quality issues or sampling noise.
No evidence supports the existence of concentration risk, operational bias, or governance gaps in incident routing or allocation across categories or personnel.
summary: The analysis conclusively shows that incident distribution across categories—and across assignees—is perfectly uniform, with exactly 100 incidents per category and per assignee out of a total of 500. This eliminates concerns about categorical imbalance, routing bias, or uneven workload pressure as potential drivers of service performance variation. The observed uniformity suggests either a tightly controlled assignment policy or a synthetic or highly curated dataset; in either case, no corrective action related to distribution imbalance is warranted. Next focus should shift to evaluating whether uniform assignment aligns with actual incident complexity, resolution time, or business impact across categories.
